message.content: Adversaries may employ downgrade attacks to impair defenses by using outdated or vulnerable versions of system features that lack updated security controls. For instance, they might execute an older version of PowerShell that does not support Script Block Logging (SBL) to avoid detection while running malicious scripts. This technique can also involve downgrading command and scripting interpreters or network protocols, potentially enabling Adversary-in-the-Middle attacks. The impact of such attacks includes reduced visibility and detection capabilities, allowing adversaries to execute malicious activities undetected. Common exploitation methods involve identifying and exploiting systems that permit the use of older, less secure versions of software or protocols. Mitigation strategies include enforcing the use of up-to-date software versions, implementing strict version control policies, and monitoring for attempts to use outdated system features. Additionally, network segmentation and robust logging practices can help detect and prevent downgrade attacks.
